Generally, but not always, this tablet will be above the tablet dedicated to Tudigong. This tablet reads {{zh|labels=no|t=天官賜福|s=天官赐福}}. * Some houses will have a tablet at or near the gate which reads {{zh|labels=no|t=門官福神|s=门官福神}} "this tablet is dedicated to the [[:en:Menshen|Door Gods]]". * One outside the house at the front door on the ground, dedicated to [[Tudigong]], an [[:en:earth god|Earth Deity]]. This tablet usually reads {{zh|t=門口土地財神 |s=门口土地财神}} (less commonly {{zh|labels=no|t=門口土地福神|s=门口土地福神}}). * One in the kitchen, dedicated to [[:en:Kitchen God|Zao Jun]], the kitchen god, which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=定福灶君}}. * One which is dedicated to the [[Landlord deity|Landlord god]], Dizhu Shen (similar to Tudigong but not the same). This tablet comes in several forms: the simple form which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=地主神位}}, or a longer, more complex form which comprises two couplets commonly reading {{zh|labels=no|t=前后地主財神，五方五土龍神|s=前後地财神，五方五土龙神}}. * Two in the house, usually at least one in the living room. These tablets will usually be put in a cabinet, similar to a Japanese ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]'' household shrine, and they will be usually for a family's ancestors and some other deity which may or may not be represented by a spirit tablet. In their most simple form the spirit tablets can simply be a piece of red paper with the words written vertically (in mainland China and in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]). More complex forms exist; these could be full, small shrines made of tile, wood, metal or other material; statues and attendants with text; small posters with incense places; and so on. A common form of the tablet for Tudigong (as seen in [[:en:Guangdong|Guangdong]], [[:en:China|China]]), for example, consists of a baked tile which has the core text of the tablet {{zh|labels=no|c=門口土地財神}}, flanked by two additional couplets reading {{zh|labels=no|t=戶納千祥， 門迎百福|s=户纳千祥， 门迎百福}}) meaning something close to "May my household welcome a great deal of auspiciousness, may my doors welcome hundreds of blessings". In [[Taoism]], spirit tablets are often used for ancestors. Sometimes spirit tablets are found before or below statues of deities, which represent the enclosed spirit of the deity. {{Multiple image| direction=horizontal| width=| footer=| width1=200| image1=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke front side.jpg| alt1=| caption1=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (front side)| width2=216| image2=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke back side.jpg| alt2=| caption2=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (back side) "Itagaki may die, but liberty never!"}} In [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]], spirit tablets, known as “lotus seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=蓮位}}) for the dead and “prosperity seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=祿位}}) for the living, are used in the same manner for ancestors, wandering spirits, demons, [[:en:Hungry ghosts|Hungry ghosts]], and the living (for the perpetual or temporary blessing of the donor). Temporary tablets in the form of paper are common around the time of Qingming and Ullambana dharma festivals, which are incinerated en masse at the culmination of these services. In [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]], tablets are used in [[:en:Funeral rites|Funeral rites]] and stored in the home ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]''. Tablets are also common in [[:en:buddhist temples in japan|Japanese temples]]. In Korean culture, spirit tablets are of great importance in ancestral rites called ''[[:en:Jesa|Jesa]]'', as they are the centerpieces of food offerings and represent the spiritual presence of the deceased. ==Gallery== File:HongKongSpiritSeat.jpg|A doorway spirit tablet dedicated to [[Tudigong]] in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]. File:Memorial Tablet for Spirits in Pingtung County Taiwan.JPG|Tablet in [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] File:祖先牌位.jpg|A stone tablet of a tombstone
(Name has been blurred for protection) File:塔婆供養.jpg|Memorial tablets used in [[:en:Nichiren Shoshu|Nichiren Shoshu]] Buddhism File:平平輔の位牌.JPG|A Japanese spirit tablet in a [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] File:Bài vị.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to emperor [[:en:Lê Trang Tông|Lê Trang Tông]] File:Bài vị Võ Văn Dũng.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Võ Văn Dũng|Võ Văn Dũng]] File:Dai Viet Ancestral Tablets (15242593880).jpg|A Japanese ''[[sotoba]]'' tablet dedicated to ancestors in [[:en:Hoian|Hoian]], [[:en:Vietnam|Vietnam]] ==See also== * [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|Ancestor veneration in China]] * [[:en:Veneration of the dead|Veneration of the dead]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] * [[:en:Chinese lineage associations|Chinese lineage associations]] and [[:en:Kongsi|Kongsi]] * [[:en:Chinese kinship|Chinese kinship]] * [[Shigandang]] * [[:en:Ullambana|Ullambana]] * [[:en:Zhizha|Zhizha]] and [[:en:Religious goods store|Religious goods store]] * [[:en:Zhong Yuan Festival|Zhong Yuan Festival]] * [[:en:Zupu|Zupu]] and [[:en:Chinese kin|Chinese kin]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Spirit tablets}}]] ]] {{Religious Confucianism}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancestral shrines]] [[Category:Filial piety]] [[Category:Practices in Chinese folk religion]] [[Category:Religion in Japan]] [[Category:Religion in Korea]] [[Category:Religious Confucianism]] [[Category:Traditional rituals of East Asia]] }} [[sn:Spirit tablet]] [[en:Spirit tablet]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[cdo:Sìng-ciō-bà̤]] {{Commons category|Spirit tablets}} [[fr:Tablette ancestrale]] [[gan:神主牌]] [[id:Papan arwah]] [[it:Tavoletta degli spiriti]] [[ja:位牌]] [[ko:위패]] [[ms:Papan roh]] [[nl:Vooroudertablet]] [[no:Shenzhupai]] [[pl:Tabliczka przodka]] [[simple:Spirit tablet]] [[th:ป้ายวิญญาณ]] [[tr:Ruh tableti]] [[uk:Таблички предків]] [[zh:牌位]] [[zh-classical:神位]] [[zh-yue:神主牌]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Ritual object in East Asian ancestor veneration}} {{Use American English|date = January 2019}} {{Use mdy dates|date = January 2019}} {{Infobox Chinese | pic = YuKiuAncestralHall06.jpg | piccap = Spirit tablets for ancestors in Hong Kong | c = 神主牌 | j = san4 zyu2 paai4 | y = sàhn jyú pàaih | p = shénzhǔpái | poj = sîn-chú-pâi | l = spirit master sign | kanji = 位牌 | revhep = ihai | hangul = 1. 위패
2. 신위 | hanja = 1. 位牌
2. 神位 | qn = bài vị | chuhan = 牌位 | rr = 1. wipae
2.shinwi | c2 = 神位 | j2 = san4 wai6 | y2 = sàhn waih | p2 = shénwèi | poj2 = sîn-ūi | l2 = spirit seat | c3 = 神牌 | j3 = san4 paai4 | y3 = sàhn pàaih | p3 = shénpái | poj3 = sîn-pâi | l3 = spirit sign }} A '''spirit tablet''', '''memorial tablet''', or '''ancestral tablet'''{{cite book|last=Li|first=Xiaoxiang|title=Origins of Chinese people and customs|year=2004|publisher=Asiapac Books|location=Singapore|isbn=978-981-229-384-8|page=130|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=l0R59sYWaCoC|edition=Revised|author2=Fu, Chunjiang|author3=Goh, Geraldine|quote=ancestral tablet}}{{Dead link|date=October 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} is a [[:en:Placard|Placard]] that people used to designate the seat of a deity or past ancestor as well as to enclose it. The name of the deity or the past ancestor is usually inscribed onto the tablet. With origins in [[:en:Chinese culture|traditional Chinese culture]], the spirit tablet is a common sight in many East Asian countries, where forms of [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|ancestor veneration]] are practiced. Spirit tablets are traditional ritual objects commonly seen in temples, shrines, and household altars throughout mainland China and Taiwan.{{cite web|title=Ancestors and Deities: Chinese Spirit Tablets|url=http://anthromuseum.missouri.edu/minigalleries/chinesespirittablets/intro.shtml|work=Museum of Anthropology|publisher=University of Missouri|accessdate=19 September 2011}} ==General usage== {{No sources|date=March 2024|section}} A spirit tablet is often used for deities or [[:en:ancestors|ancestors]] (either generally or specifically: e.g. for a specific relative or for one's entire family tree). Shrines are generally found in and around households (for [[:en:Household deity|household gods]] and ancestors), in [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] for specific deities, or in [[ancestral shrine]]s for the clan's founders and specific ancestors. In each place, there are specific locations for individual spirit tablets for ancestors or one or another particular deity. A spirit tablet acts as an [[:en:Effigy|Effigy]] of a specific deity or ancestor. When used, [[:en:Incense|incense sticks]] or [[:en:joss sticks|joss sticks]] are usually burned before the tablet in some kind of [[:en:brazier|brazier]] or [[:en:Censer|incense holder]]. Sometimes fruit, tea, pastries, or other offertory items are placed near the tablet to offer food to that particular spirit or divinity. In [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese folk religion]] a household will have one or more tablets for specific deities and family ancestors: * One near the front door, and at or around eye level, dedicated to the [[:en:Jade Emperor|Jade Emperor]]. Generally, but not always, this tablet will be above the tablet dedicated to Tudigong. This tablet reads {{zh|labels=no|t=天官賜福|s=天官赐福}}. * Some houses will have a tablet at or near the gate which reads {{zh|labels=no|t=門官福神|s=门官福神}} "this tablet is dedicated to the [[:en:Menshen|Door Gods]]". * One outside the house at the front door on the ground, dedicated to [[Tudigong]], an [[:en:earth god|Earth Deity]]. This tablet usually reads {{zh|t=門口土地財神 |s=门口土地财神}} (less commonly {{zh|labels=no|t=門口土地福神|s=门口土地福神}}). * One in the kitchen, dedicated to [[:en:Kitchen God|Zao Jun]], the kitchen god, which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=定福灶君}}. * One which is dedicated to the [[Landlord deity|Landlord god]], Dizhu Shen (similar to Tudigong but not the same). This tablet comes in several forms: the simple form which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=地主神位}}, or a longer, more complex form which comprises two couplets commonly reading {{zh|labels=no|t=前后地主財神，五方五土龍神|s=前後地财神，五方五土龙神}}. * Two in the house, usually at least one in the living room. These tablets will usually be put in a cabinet, similar to a Japanese ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]'' household shrine, and they will be usually for a family's ancestors and some other deity which may or may not be represented by a spirit tablet. In their most simple form the spirit tablets can simply be a piece of red paper with the words written vertically (in mainland China and in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]). More complex forms exist; these could be full, small shrines made of tile, wood, metal or other material; statues and attendants with text; small posters with incense places; and so on. A common form of the tablet for Tudigong (as seen in [[:en:Guangdong|Guangdong]], [[:en:China|China]]), for example, consists of a baked tile which has the core text of the tablet {{zh|labels=no|c=門口土地財神}}, flanked by two additional couplets reading {{zh|labels=no|t=戶納千祥， 門迎百福|s=户纳千祥， 门迎百福}}) meaning something close to "May my household welcome a great deal of auspiciousness, may my doors welcome hundreds of blessings". In [[Taoism]], spirit tablets are often used for ancestors. Sometimes spirit tablets are found before or below statues of deities, which represent the enclosed spirit of the deity. {{Multiple image| direction=horizontal| width=| footer=| width1=200| image1=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke front side.jpg| alt1=| caption1=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (front side)| width2=216| image2=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke back side.jpg| alt2=| caption2=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (back side) "Itagaki may die, but liberty never!"}} In [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]], spirit tablets, known as “lotus seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=蓮位}}) for the dead and “prosperity seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=祿位}}) for the living, are used in the same manner for ancestors, wandering spirits, demons, [[:en:Hungry ghosts|Hungry ghosts]], and the living (for the perpetual or temporary blessing of the donor). Temporary tablets in the form of paper are common around the time of Qingming and Ullambana dharma festivals, which are incinerated en masse at the culmination of these services. In [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]], tablets are used in [[:en:Funeral rites|Funeral rites]] and stored in the home ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]''. Tablets are also common in [[:en:buddhist temples in japan|Japanese temples]]. In Korean culture, spirit tablets are of great importance in ancestral rites called ''[[:en:Jesa|Jesa]]'', as they are the centerpieces of food offerings and represent the spiritual presence of the deceased. ==Gallery== File:HongKongSpiritSeat.jpg|A doorway spirit tablet dedicated to [[Tudigong]] in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]. File:Memorial Tablet for Spirits in Pingtung County Taiwan.JPG|Tablet in [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] File:祖先牌位.jpg|A stone tablet of a tombstone
(Name has been blurred for protection) File:塔婆供養.jpg|Memorial tablets used in [[:en:Nichiren Shoshu|Nichiren Shoshu]] Buddhism File:平平輔の位牌.JPG|A Japanese spirit tablet in a [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] File:Bài vị.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to emperor [[:en:Lê Trang Tông|Lê Trang Tông]] File:Bài vị Võ Văn Dũng.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Võ Văn Dũng|Võ Văn Dũng]] File:Dai Viet Ancestral Tablets (15242593880).jpg|A Japanese ''[[sotoba]]'' tablet dedicated to ancestors in [[:en:Hoian|Hoian]], [[:en:Vietnam|Vietnam]] ==See also== * [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|Ancestor veneration in China]] * [[:en:Veneration of the dead|Veneration of the dead]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] * [[:en:Chinese lineage associations|Chinese lineage associations]] and [[:en:Kongsi|Kongsi]] * [[:en:Chinese kinship|Chinese kinship]] * [[:en:Shigandang|Shigandang]] * [[:en:Ullambana|Ullambana]] * [[:en:Zhizha|Zhizha]] and [[:en:Religious goods store|Religious goods store]] * [[:en:Zhong Yuan Festival|Zhong Yuan Festival]] * [[:en:Zupu|Zupu]] and [[:en:Chinese kin|Chinese kin]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Spirit tablets}}]] ]] {{Religious Confucianism}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancestral shrines]] [[Category:Filial piety]] [[Category:Practices in Chinese folk religion]] [[Category:Religion in Japan]] [[Category:Religion in Korea]] [[Category:Religious Confucianism]] [[Category:Traditional rituals of East Asia]] }} [[sn:Spirit tablet]] [[en:Spirit tablet]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[cdo:Sìng-ciō-bà̤]] {{Commons category|Spirit tablets}} [[fr:Tablette ancestrale]] [[gan:神主牌]] [[id:Papan arwah]] [[it:Tavoletta degli spiriti]] [[ja:位牌]] [[ko:위패]] [[ms:Papan roh]] [[nl:Vooroudertablet]] [[no:Shenzhupai]] [[pl:Tabliczka przodka]] [[simple:Spirit tablet]] [[th:ป้ายวิญญาณ]] [[tr:Ruh tableti]] [[uk:Таблички предків]] [[zh:牌位]] [[zh-classical:神位]] [[zh-yue:神主牌]]

{{Short description|Ritual object in East Asian ancestor veneration}} {{Use American English|date = January 2019}} {{Use mdy dates|date = January 2019}} {{Infobox Chinese | pic = YuKiuAncestralHall06.jpg | piccap = Spirit tablets for ancestors in Hong Kong | c = 神主牌 | j = san4 zyu2 paai4 | y = sàhn jyú pàaih | p = shénzhǔpái | poj = sîn-chú-pâi | l = spirit master sign | kanji = 位牌 | revhep = ihai | hangul = 1. 위패
2. 신위 | hanja = 1. 位牌
2. 神位 | qn = bài vị | chuhan = 牌位 | rr = 1. wipae
2.shinwi | c2 = 神位 | j2 = san4 wai6 | y2 = sàhn waih | p2 = shénwèi | poj2 = sîn-ūi | l2 = spirit seat | c3 = 神牌 | j3 = san4 paai4 | y3 = sàhn pàaih | p3 = shénpái | poj3 = sîn-pâi | l3 = spirit sign }} A '''spirit tablet''', '''memorial tablet''', or '''ancestral tablet'''{{cite book|last=Li|first=Xiaoxiang|title=Origins of Chinese people and customs|year=2004|publisher=Asiapac Books|location=Singapore|isbn=978-981-229-384-8|page=130|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=l0R59sYWaCoC|edition=Revised|author2=Fu, Chunjiang|author3=Goh, Geraldine|quote=ancestral tablet}}{{Dead link|date=October 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} is a [[:en:Placard|Placard]] that people used to designate the seat of a deity or past ancestor as well as to enclose it. The name of the deity or the past ancestor is usually inscribed onto the tablet. With origins in [[:en:Chinese culture|traditional Chinese culture]], the spirit tablet is a common sight in many East Asian countries, where forms of [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|ancestor veneration]] are practiced. Spirit tablets are traditional ritual objects commonly seen in temples, shrines, and household altars throughout mainland China and Taiwan.{{cite web|title=Ancestors and Deities: Chinese Spirit Tablets|url=http://anthromuseum.missouri.edu/minigalleries/chinesespirittablets/intro.shtml|work=Museum of Anthropology|publisher=University of Missouri|accessdate=19 September 2011}} ==General usage== {{No sources|date=March 2024|section}} A spirit tablet is often used for deities or [[:en:ancestors|ancestors]] (either generally or specifically: e.g. for a specific relative or for one's entire family tree). Shrines are generally found in and around households (for [[:en:Household deity|household gods]] and ancestors), in [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] for specific deities, or in [[ancestral shrine]]s for the clan's founders and specific ancestors. In each place, there are specific locations for individual spirit tablets for ancestors or one or another particular deity. A spirit tablet acts as an [[:en:Effigy|Effigy]] of a specific deity or ancestor. When used, [[:en:Incense|incense sticks]] or [[:en:joss sticks|joss sticks]] are usually burned before the tablet in some kind of [[:en:brazier|brazier]] or [[:en:Censer|incense holder]]. Sometimes fruit, tea, pastries, or other offertory items are placed near the tablet to offer food to that particular spirit or divinity. In [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese folk religion]] a household will have one or more tablets for specific deities and family ancestors: * One near the front door, and at or around eye level, dedicated to the [[:en:Jade Emperor|Jade Emperor]]. Generally, but not always, this tablet will be above the tablet dedicated to Tudigong. This tablet reads {{zh|labels=no|t=天官賜福|s=天官赐福}}. * Some houses will have a tablet at or near the gate which reads {{zh|labels=no|t=門官福神|s=门官福神}} "this tablet is dedicated to the [[:en:Menshen|Door Gods]]". * One outside the house at the front door on the ground, dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]], an [[:en:earth god|Earth Deity]]. This tablet usually reads {{zh|t=門口土地財神 |s=门口土地财神}} (less commonly {{zh|labels=no|t=門口土地福神|s=门口土地福神}}). * One in the kitchen, dedicated to [[:en:Kitchen God|Zao Jun]], the kitchen god, which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=定福灶君}}. * One which is dedicated to the [[Landlord deity|Landlord god]], Dizhu Shen (similar to Tudigong but not the same). This tablet comes in several forms: the simple form which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=地主神位}}, or a longer, more complex form which comprises two couplets commonly reading {{zh|labels=no|t=前后地主財神，五方五土龍神|s=前後地财神，五方五土龙神}}. * Two in the house, usually at least one in the living room. These tablets will usually be put in a cabinet, similar to a Japanese ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]'' household shrine, and they will be usually for a family's ancestors and some other deity which may or may not be represented by a spirit tablet. In their most simple form the spirit tablets can simply be a piece of red paper with the words written vertically (in mainland China and in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]). More complex forms exist; these could be full, small shrines made of tile, wood, metal or other material; statues and attendants with text; small posters with incense places; and so on. A common form of the tablet for Tudigong (as seen in [[:en:Guangdong|Guangdong]], [[:en:China|China]]), for example, consists of a baked tile which has the core text of the tablet {{zh|labels=no|c=門口土地財神}}, flanked by two additional couplets reading {{zh|labels=no|t=戶納千祥， 門迎百福|s=户纳千祥， 门迎百福}}) meaning something close to "May my household welcome a great deal of auspiciousness, may my doors welcome hundreds of blessings". In [[Taoism]], spirit tablets are often used for ancestors. Sometimes spirit tablets are found before or below statues of deities, which represent the enclosed spirit of the deity. {{Multiple image| direction=horizontal| width=| footer=| width1=200| image1=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke front side.jpg| alt1=| caption1=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (front side)| width2=216| image2=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke back side.jpg| alt2=| caption2=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (back side) "Itagaki may die, but liberty never!"}} In [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]], spirit tablets, known as “lotus seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=蓮位}}) for the dead and “prosperity seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=祿位}}) for the living, are used in the same manner for ancestors, wandering spirits, demons, [[:en:Hungry ghosts|Hungry ghosts]], and the living (for the perpetual or temporary blessing of the donor). Temporary tablets in the form of paper are common around the time of Qingming and Ullambana dharma festivals, which are incinerated en masse at the culmination of these services. In [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]], tablets are used in [[:en:Funeral rites|Funeral rites]] and stored in the home ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]''. Tablets are also common in [[:en:buddhist temples in japan|Japanese temples]]. In Korean culture, spirit tablets are of great importance in ancestral rites called ''[[:en:Jesa|Jesa]]'', as they are the centerpieces of food offerings and represent the spiritual presence of the deceased. ==Gallery== File:HongKongSpiritSeat.jpg|A doorway spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]. File:Memorial Tablet for Spirits in Pingtung County Taiwan.JPG|Tablet in [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] File:祖先牌位.jpg|A stone tablet of a tombstone
(Name has been blurred for protection) File:塔婆供養.jpg|Memorial tablets used in [[:en:Nichiren Shoshu|Nichiren Shoshu]] Buddhism File:平平輔の位牌.JPG|A Japanese spirit tablet in a [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] File:Bài vị.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to emperor [[:en:Lê Trang Tông|Lê Trang Tông]] File:Bài vị Võ Văn Dũng.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Võ Văn Dũng|Võ Văn Dũng]] File:Dai Viet Ancestral Tablets (15242593880).jpg|A Japanese ''[[:en:sotoba|sotoba]]'' tablet dedicated to ancestors in [[:en:Hoian|Hoian]], [[:en:Vietnam|Vietnam]] ==See also== * [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|Ancestor veneration in China]] * [[:en:Veneration of the dead|Veneration of the dead]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] * [[:en:Chinese lineage associations|Chinese lineage associations]] and [[:en:Kongsi|Kongsi]] * [[:en:Chinese kinship|Chinese kinship]] * [[:en:Shigandang|Shigandang]] * [[:en:Ullambana|Ullambana]] * [[:en:Zhizha|Zhizha]] and [[:en:Religious goods store|Religious goods store]] * [[:en:Zhong Yuan Festival|Zhong Yuan Festival]] * [[:en:Zupu|Zupu]] and [[:en:Chinese kin|Chinese kin]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Spirit tablets}}]] ]] {{Religious Confucianism}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancestral shrines]] [[Category:Filial piety]] [[Category:Practices in Chinese folk religion]] [[Category:Religion in Japan]] [[Category:Religion in Korea]] [[Category:Religious Confucianism]] [[Category:Traditional rituals of East Asia]] }} [[sn:Spirit tablet]] [[en:Spirit tablet]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[cdo:Sìng-ciō-bà̤]] {{Commons category|Spirit tablets}} [[fr:Tablette ancestrale]] [[gan:神主牌]] [[id:Papan arwah]] [[it:Tavoletta degli spiriti]] [[ja:位牌]] [[ko:위패]] [[ms:Papan roh]] [[nl:Vooroudertablet]] [[no:Shenzhupai]] [[pl:Tabliczka przodka]] [[simple:Spirit tablet]] [[th:ป้ายวิญญาณ]] [[tr:Ruh tableti]] [[uk:Таблички предків]] [[zh:牌位]] [[zh-classical:神位]] [[zh-yue:神主牌]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1375504]] Wikidata name: spirit tablet [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Ritual object in East Asian ancestor veneration}} {{Use American English|date = January 2019}} {{Use mdy dates|date = January 2019}} {{Infobox Chinese | pic = YuKiuAncestralHall06.jpg | piccap = Spirit tablets for ancestors in Hong Kong | c = 神主牌 | j = san4 zyu2 paai4 | y = sàhn jyú pàaih | p = shénzhǔpái | poj = sîn-chú-pâi | l = spirit master sign | kanji = 位牌 | revhep = ihai | hangul = 1. 위패

A common form of the tablet for Tudigong (as seen in [[:en:Guangdong|Guangdong]], [[:en:China|China]]), for example, consists of a baked tile which has the core text of the tablet {{zh|labels=no|c=門口土地財神}}, flanked by two additional couplets reading {{zh|labels=no|t=戶納千祥， 門迎百福|s=户纳千祥， 门迎百福}}) meaning something close to "May my household welcome a great deal of auspiciousness, may my doors welcome hundreds of blessings". In [[Taoism]], spirit tablets are often used for ancestors. Sometimes spirit tablets are found before or below statues of deities, which represent the enclosed spirit of the deity. {{Multiple image| direction=horizontal| width=| footer=| width1=200| image1=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke front side.jpg| alt1=| caption1=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (front side)| width2=216| image2=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke back side.jpg| alt2=| caption2=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (back side) "Itagaki may die, but liberty never!"}} In [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]], spirit tablets, known as “lotus seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=蓮位}}) for the dead and “prosperity seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=祿位}}) for the living, are used in the same manner for ancestors, wandering spirits, demons, [[:en:Hungry ghosts|Hungry ghosts]], and the living (for the perpetual or temporary blessing of the donor). Temporary tablets in the form of paper are common around the time of Qingming and Ullambana dharma festivals, which are incinerated en masse at the culmination of these services. In [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]], tablets are used in [[:en:Funeral rites|Funeral rites]] and stored in the home ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]''. Tablets are also common in [[:en:buddhist temples in japan|Japanese temples]]. In Korean culture, spirit tablets are of great importance in ancestral rites called ''[[:en:Jesa|Jesa]]'', as they are the centerpieces of food offerings and represent the spiritual presence of the deceased. ==Gallery== File:HongKongSpiritSeat.jpg|A doorway spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]. File:Memorial Tablet for Spirits in Pingtung County Taiwan.JPG|Tablet in [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] File:祖先牌位.jpg|A stone tablet of a tombstone
(Name has been blurred for protection) File:塔婆供養.jpg|Memorial tablets used in [[:en:Nichiren Shoshu|Nichiren Shoshu]] Buddhism File:平平輔の位牌.JPG|A Japanese spirit tablet in a [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] File:Bài vị.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to emperor [[:en:Lê Trang Tông|Lê Trang Tông]] File:Bài vị Võ Văn Dũng.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Võ Văn Dũng|Võ Văn Dũng]] File:Dai Viet Ancestral Tablets (15242593880).jpg|A Japanese ''[[:en:sotoba|sotoba]]'' tablet dedicated to ancestors in [[:en:Hoian|Hoian]], [[:en:Vietnam|Vietnam]] ==See also== * [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|Ancestor veneration in China]] * [[:en:Veneration of the dead|Veneration of the dead]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] * [[:en:Chinese lineage associations|Chinese lineage associations]] and [[:en:Kongsi|Kongsi]] * [[:en:Chinese kinship|Chinese kinship]] * [[:en:Shigandang|Shigandang]] * [[:en:Ullambana|Ullambana]] * [[:en:Zhizha|Zhizha]] and [[:en:Religious goods store|Religious goods store]] * [[:en:Zhong Yuan Festival|Zhong Yuan Festival]] * [[:en:Zupu|Zupu]] and [[:en:Chinese kin|Chinese kin]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Spirit tablets}}]] {{Religious Confucianism}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancestral shrines]] [[Category:Filial piety]] [[Category:Practices in Chinese folk religion]] [[Category:Religion in Japan]] [[Category:Religion in Korea]] [[Category:Religious Confucianism]] [[Category:Traditional rituals of East Asia]] }} [[sn:Spirit tablet]] [[en:Spirit tablet]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[cdo:Sìng-ciō-bà̤]] {{Commons category|Spirit tablets}} [[fr:Tablette ancestrale]] [[gan:神主牌]] [[id:Papan arwah]] [[it:Tavoletta degli spiriti]] [[ja:位牌]] [[ko:위패]] [[ms:Papan roh]] [[nl:Vooroudertablet]] [[no:Shenzhupai]] [[pl:Tabliczka przodka]] [[simple:Spirit tablet]] [[th:ป้ายวิญญาณ]] [[tr:Ruh tableti]] [[uk:Таблички предків]] [[zh:牌位]] [[zh-classical:神位]] [[zh-yue:神主牌]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1375504]] Wikidata name: spirit tablet [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Ritual object in East Asian ancestor veneration}} {{Use American English|date = January 2019}} {{Use mdy dates|date = January 2019}} {{Infobox Chinese | pic = YuKiuAncestralHall06.jpg | piccap = Spirit tablets for ancestors in Hong Kong | c = 神主牌 | j = san4 zyu2 paai4 | y = sàhn jyú pàaih | p = shénzhǔpái | poj = sîn-chú-pâi | l = spirit master sign | kanji = 位牌 | revhep = ihai | hangul = 1. 위패
2. 신위 | hanja = 1. 位牌
2. 神位 | qn = bài vị | chuhan = 牌位 | rr = 1. wipae
2.shinwi | c2 = 神位 | j2 = san4 wai6 | y2 = sàhn waih | p2 = shénwèi | poj2 = sîn-ūi | l2 = spirit seat | c3 = 神牌 | j3 = san4 paai4 | y3 = sàhn pàaih | p3 = shénpái | poj3 = sîn-pâi | l3 = spirit sign }} A '''spirit tablet''', '''memorial tablet''', or '''ancestral tablet'''{{cite book|last=Li|first=Xiaoxiang|title=Origins of Chinese people and customs|year=2004|publisher=Asiapac Books|location=Singapore|isbn=978-981-229-384-8|page=130|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=l0R59sYWaCoC|edition=Revised|author2=Fu, Chunjiang|author3=Goh, Geraldine|quote=ancestral tablet}}{{Dead link|date=October 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} is a [[:en:Placard|Placard]] that people used to designate the seat of a deity or past ancestor as well as to enclose it. The name of the deity or the past ancestor is usually inscribed onto the tablet. With origins in [[:en:Chinese culture|traditional Chinese culture]], the spirit tablet is a common sight in many East Asian countries, where forms of [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|ancestor veneration]] are practiced. Spirit tablets are traditional ritual objects commonly seen in temples, shrines, and household altars throughout mainland China and Taiwan.{{cite web|title=Ancestors and Deities: Chinese Spirit Tablets|url=http://anthromuseum.missouri.edu/minigalleries/chinesespirittablets/intro.shtml|work=Museum of Anthropology|publisher=University of Missouri|accessdate=19 September 2011}} ==General usage== {{No sources|date=March 2024|section}} A spirit tablet is often used for deities or [[:en:ancestors|ancestors]] (either generally or specifically: e.g. for a specific relative or for one's entire family tree). Shrines are generally found in and around households (for [[:en:Household deity|household gods]] and ancestors), in [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] for specific deities, or in [[ancestral shrine]]s for the clan's founders and specific ancestors. In each place, there are specific locations for individual spirit tablets for ancestors or one or another particular deity. A spirit tablet acts as an [[:en:Effigy|Effigy]] of a specific deity or ancestor. When used, [[:en:Incense|incense sticks]] or [[:en:joss sticks|joss sticks]] are usually burned before the tablet in some kind of [[:en:brazier|brazier]] or [[:en:Censer|incense holder]]. Sometimes fruit, tea, pastries, or other offertory items are placed near the tablet to offer food to that particular spirit or divinity. In [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese folk religion]] a household will have one or more tablets for specific deities and family ancestors: * One near the front door, and at or around eye level, dedicated to the [[:en:Jade Emperor|Jade Emperor]]. Generally, but not always, this tablet will be above the tablet dedicated to Tudigong. This tablet reads {{zh|labels=no|t=天官賜福|s=天官赐福}}. * Some houses will have a tablet at or near the gate which reads {{zh|labels=no|t=門官福神|s=门官福神}} "this tablet is dedicated to the [[:en:Menshen|Door Gods]]". * One outside the house at the front door on the ground, dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]], an [[:en:earth god|Earth Deity]]. This tablet usually reads {{zh|t=門口土地財神 |s=门口土地财神}} (less commonly {{zh|labels=no|t=門口土地福神|s=门口土地福神}}). * One in the kitchen, dedicated to [[:en:Kitchen God|Zao Jun]], the kitchen god, which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=定福灶君}}. * One which is dedicated to the [[Landlord deity|Landlord god]], Dizhu Shen (similar to Tudigong but not the same). This tablet comes in several forms: the simple form which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=地主神位}}, or a longer, more complex form which comprises two couplets commonly reading {{zh|labels=no|t=前后地主財神，五方五土龍神|s=前後地财神，五方五土龙神}}. * Two in the house, usually at least one in the living room. These tablets will usually be put in a cabinet, similar to a Japanese ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]'' household shrine, and they will be usually for a family's ancestors and some other deity which may or may not be represented by a spirit tablet. In their most simple form the spirit tablets can simply be a piece of red paper with the words written vertically (in mainland China and in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]). More complex forms exist; these could be full, small shrines made of tile, wood, metal or other material; statues and attendants with text; small posters with incense places; and so on. A common form of the tablet for Tudigong (as seen in [[:en:Guangdong|Guangdong]], [[:en:China|China]]), for example, consists of a baked tile which has the core text of the tablet {{zh|labels=no|c=門口土地財神}}, flanked by two additional couplets reading {{zh|labels=no|t=戶納千祥， 門迎百福|s=户纳千祥， 门迎百福}}) meaning something close to "May my household welcome a great deal of auspiciousness, may my doors welcome hundreds of blessings". In [[Taoism]], spirit tablets are often used for ancestors. Sometimes spirit tablets are found before or below statues of deities, which represent the enclosed spirit of the deity. {{Multiple image| direction=horizontal| width=| footer=| width1=200| image1=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke front side.jpg| alt1=| caption1=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (front side)| width2=216| image2=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke back side.jpg| alt2=| caption2=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (back side) "Itagaki may die, but liberty never!"}} In [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]], spirit tablets, known as “lotus seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=蓮位}}) for the dead and “prosperity seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=祿位}}) for the living, are used in the same manner for ancestors, wandering spirits, demons, [[:en:Hungry ghosts|Hungry ghosts]], and the living (for the perpetual or temporary blessing of the donor). Temporary tablets in the form of paper are common around the time of Qingming and Ullambana dharma festivals, which are incinerated en masse at the culmination of these services. In [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]], tablets are used in [[:en:Funeral rites|Funeral rites]] and stored in the home ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]''. Tablets are also common in [[:en:buddhist temples in japan|Japanese temples]]. In Korean culture, spirit tablets are of great importance in ancestral rites called ''[[:en:Jesa|Jesa]]'', as they are the centerpieces of food offerings and represent the spiritual presence of the deceased. ==Gallery== File:HongKongSpiritSeat.jpg|A doorway spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]. File:Memorial Tablet for Spirits in Pingtung County Taiwan.JPG|Tablet in [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] File:祖先牌位.jpg|A stone tablet of a tombstone
(Name has been blurred for protection) File:塔婆供養.jpg|Memorial tablets used in [[:en:Nichiren Shoshu|Nichiren Shoshu]] Buddhism File:平平輔の位牌.JPG|A Japanese spirit tablet in a [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] File:Bài vị.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to emperor [[:en:Lê Trang Tông|Lê Trang Tông]] File:Bài vị Võ Văn Dũng.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Võ Văn Dũng|Võ Văn Dũng]] File:Dai Viet Ancestral Tablets (15242593880).jpg|A Japanese ''[[:en:sotoba|sotoba]]'' tablet dedicated to ancestors in [[:en:Hoian|Hoian]], [[:en:Vietnam|Vietnam]] ==See also== * [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|Ancestor veneration in China]] * [[:en:Veneration of the dead|Veneration of the dead]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] * [[:en:Chinese lineage associations|Chinese lineage associations]] and [[:en:Kongsi|Kongsi]] * [[:en:Chinese kinship|Chinese kinship]] * [[:en:Shigandang|Shigandang]] * [[:en:Ullambana|Ullambana]] * [[:en:Zhizha|Zhizha]] and [[:en:Religious goods store|Religious goods store]] * [[:en:Zhong Yuan Festival|Zhong Yuan Festival]] * [[:en:Zupu|Zupu]] and [[:en:Chinese kin|Chinese kin]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Spirit tablets}}]] {{Religious Confucianism}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancestral shrines]] [[Category:Filial piety]] [[Category:Practices in Chinese folk religion]] [[Category:Religion in Japan]] [[Category:Religion in Korea]] [[Category:Religious Confucianism]] [[Category:Traditional rituals of East Asia]] }} [[sn:Spirit tablet]] [[en:Spirit tablet]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[cdo:Sìng-ciō-bà̤]] {{Commons category|Spirit tablets}} [[fr:Tablette ancestrale]] [[gan:神主牌]] [[id:Papan arwah]] [[it:Tavoletta degli spiriti]] [[ja:位牌]] [[ko:위패]] [[ms:Papan roh]] [[nl:Vooroudertablet]] [[no:Shenzhupai]] [[pl:Tabliczka przodka]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]]Spirit tablet]] [[th:ป้ายวิญญาณ]] [[tr:Ruh tableti]] [[uk:Таблички предків]] [[zh:牌位]] [[zh-classical:神位]] [[zh-yue:神主牌]]

2. 신위 | hanja = 1. 位牌
2. 神位 | qn = bài vị | chuhan = 牌位 | rr = 1. wipae
2.shinwi | c2 = 神位 | j2 = san4 wai6 | y2 = sàhn waih | p2 = shénwèi | poj2 = sîn-ūi | l2 = spirit seat | c3 = 神牌 | j3 = san4 paai4 | y3 = sàhn pàaih | p3 = shénpái | poj3 = sîn-pâi | l3 = spirit sign }} A '''spirit tablet''', '''memorial tablet''', or '''ancestral tablet'''{{cite book|last=Li|first=Xiaoxiang|title=Origins of Chinese people and customs|year=2004|publisher=Asiapac Books|location=Singapore|isbn=978-981-229-384-8|page=130|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=l0R59sYWaCoC|edition=Revised|author2=Fu, Chunjiang|author3=Goh, Geraldine|quote=ancestral tablet}}{{Dead link|date=October 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} is a [[:en:Placard|Placard]] that people used to designate the seat of a deity or past ancestor as well as to enclose it. The name of the deity or the past ancestor is usually inscribed onto the tablet. With origins in [[:en:Chinese culture|traditional Chinese culture]], the spirit tablet is a common sight in many East Asian countries, where forms of [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|ancestor veneration]] are practiced. Spirit tablets are traditional ritual objects commonly seen in temples, shrines, and household altars throughout mainland China and Taiwan.{{cite web|title=Ancestors and Deities: Chinese Spirit Tablets|url=http://anthromuseum.missouri.edu/minigalleries/chinesespirittablets/intro.shtml|work=Museum of Anthropology|publisher=University of Missouri|accessdate=19 September 2011}} ==General usage== {{No sources|date=March 2024|section}} A spirit tablet is often used for deities or [[:en:ancestors|ancestors]] (either generally or specifically: e.g. for a specific relative or for one's entire family tree). Shrines are generally found in and around households (for [[:en:Household deity|household gods]] and ancestors), in [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] for specific deities, or in [[ancestral shrine]]s for the clan's founders and specific ancestors. In each place, there are specific locations for individual spirit tablets for ancestors or one or another particular deity. A spirit tablet acts as an [[:en:Effigy|Effigy]] of a specific deity or ancestor. When used, [[:en:Incense|incense sticks]] or [[:en:joss sticks|joss sticks]] are usually burned before the tablet in some kind of [[:en:brazier|brazier]] or [[:en:Censer|incense holder]]. Sometimes fruit, tea, pastries, or other offertory items are placed near the tablet to offer food to that particular spirit or divinity. In [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese folk religion]] a household will have one or more tablets for specific deities and family ancestors: * One near the front door, and at or around eye level, dedicated to the [[:en:Jade Emperor|Jade Emperor]]. Generally, but not always, this tablet will be above the tablet dedicated to Tudigong. This tablet reads {{zh|labels=no|t=天官賜福|s=天官赐福}}. * Some houses will have a tablet at or near the gate which reads {{zh|labels=no|t=門官福神|s=门官福神}} "this tablet is dedicated to the [[:en:Menshen|Door Gods]]". * One outside the house at the front door on the ground, dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]], an [[:en:earth god|Earth Deity]]. This tablet usually reads {{zh|t=門口土地財神 |s=门口土地财神}} (less commonly {{zh|labels=no|t=門口土地福神|s=门口土地福神}}). * One in the kitchen, dedicated to [[:en:Kitchen God|Zao Jun]], the kitchen god, which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=定福灶君}}. * One which is dedicated to the [[Landlord deity|Landlord god]], Dizhu Shen (similar to Tudigong but not the same). This tablet comes in several forms: the simple form which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=地主神位}}, or a longer, more complex form which comprises two couplets commonly reading {{zh|labels=no|t=前后地主財神，五方五土龍神|s=前後地财神，五方五土龙神}}. * Two in the house, usually at least one in the living room. These tablets will usually be put in a cabinet, similar to a Japanese ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]'' household shrine, and they will be usually for a family's ancestors and some other deity which may or may not be represented by a spirit tablet. In their most simple form the spirit tablets can simply be a piece of red paper with the words written vertically (in mainland China and in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]). More complex forms exist; these could be full, small shrines made of tile, wood, metal or other material; statues and attendants with text; small posters with incense places; and so on. A common form of the tablet for Tudigong (as seen in [[:en:Guangdong|Guangdong]], [[:en:China|China]]), for example, consists of a baked tile which has the core text of the tablet {{zh|labels=no|c=門口土地財神}}, flanked by two additional couplets reading {{zh|labels=no|t=戶納千祥， 門迎百福|s=户纳千祥， 门迎百福}}) meaning something close to "May my household welcome a great deal of auspiciousness, may my doors welcome hundreds of blessings". In [[Taoism]], spirit tablets are often used for ancestors. Sometimes spirit tablets are found before or below statues of deities, which represent the enclosed spirit of the deity. {{Multiple image| direction=horizontal| width=| footer=| width1=200| image1=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke front side.jpg| alt1=| caption1=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (front side)| width2=216| image2=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke back side.jpg| alt2=| caption2=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (back side) "Itagaki may die, but liberty never!"}} In [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]], spirit tablets, known as “lotus seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=蓮位}}) for the dead and “prosperity seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=祿位}}) for the living, are used in the same manner for ancestors, wandering spirits, demons, [[:en:Hungry ghosts|Hungry ghosts]], and the living (for the perpetual or temporary blessing of the donor). Temporary tablets in the form of paper are common around the time of Qingming and Ullambana dharma festivals, which are incinerated en masse at the culmination of these services. In [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]], tablets are used in [[:en:Funeral rites|Funeral rites]] and stored in the home ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]''. Tablets are also common in [[:en:buddhist temples in japan|Japanese temples]]. In Korean culture, spirit tablets are of great importance in ancestral rites called ''[[:en:Jesa|Jesa]]'', as they are the centerpieces of food offerings and represent the spiritual presence of the deceased. ==Gallery== File:HongKongSpiritSeat.jpg|A doorway spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]. File:Memorial Tablet for Spirits in Pingtung County Taiwan.JPG|Tablet in [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] File:祖先牌位.jpg|A stone tablet of a tombstone
(Name has been blurred for protection) File:塔婆供養.jpg|Memorial tablets used in [[:en:Nichiren Shoshu|Nichiren Shoshu]] Buddhism File:平平輔の位牌.JPG|A Japanese spirit tablet in a [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] File:Bài vị.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to emperor [[:en:Lê Trang Tông|Lê Trang Tông]] File:Bài vị Võ Văn Dũng.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Võ Văn Dũng|Võ Văn Dũng]] File:Dai Viet Ancestral Tablets (15242593880).jpg|A Japanese ''[[:en:sotoba|sotoba]]'' tablet dedicated to ancestors in [[:en:Hoian|Hoian]], [[:en:Vietnam|Vietnam]] ==See also== * [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|Ancestor veneration in China]] * [[:en:Veneration of the dead|Veneration of the dead]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] * [[:en:Chinese lineage associations|Chinese lineage associations]] and [[:en:Kongsi|Kongsi]] * [[:en:Chinese kinship|Chinese kinship]] * [[:en:Shigandang|Shigandang]] * [[:en:Ullambana|Ullambana]] * [[:en:Zhizha|Zhizha]] and [[:en:Religious goods store|Religious goods store]] * [[:en:Zhong Yuan Festival|Zhong Yuan Festival]] * [[:en:Zupu|Zupu]] and [[:en:Chinese kin|Chinese kin]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Spirit tablets}}]] {{Religious Confucianism}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancestral shrines]] [[Category:Filial piety]] [[Category:Practices in Chinese folk religion]] [[Category:Religion in Japan]] [[Category:Religion in Korea]] [[Category:Religious Confucianism]] [[Category:Traditional rituals of East Asia]] }} [[sn:Spirit tablet]] [[en:Spirit tablet]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[cdo:Sìng-ciō-bà̤]] {{Commons category|Spirit tablets}} [[fr:Tablette ancestrale]] [[gan:神主牌]] [[id:Papan arwah]] [[it:Tavoletta degli spiriti]] [[ja:位牌]] [[ko:위패]] [[ms:Papan roh]] [[nl:Vooroudertablet]] [[no:Shenzhupai]] [[pl:Tabliczka przodka]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Spirit tablet]] [[th:ป้ายวิญญาณ]] [[tr:Ruh tableti]] [[uk:Таблички предків]] [[zh:牌位]] [[zh-classical:神位]] [[zh-yue:神主牌]] {{wikidata link|Q1375504}}

In their most simple form the spirit tablets can simply be a piece of red paper with the words written vertically (in mainland China and in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]). More complex forms exist; these could be full, small shrines made of tile, wood, metal or other material; statues and attendants with text; small posters with incense places; and so on. A common form of the tablet for Tudigong (as seen in [[:en:Guangdong|Guangdong]], [[:en:China|China]]), for example, consists of a baked tile which has the core text of the tablet {{zh|labels=no|c=門口土地財神}}, flanked by two additional couplets reading {{zh|labels=no|t=戶納千祥， 門迎百福|s=户纳千祥， 门迎百福}}) meaning something close to "May my household welcome a great deal of auspiciousness, may my doors welcome hundreds of blessings". In [[Taoism]], spirit tablets are often used for ancestors. Sometimes spirit tablets are found before or below statues of deities, which represent the enclosed spirit of the deity. {{Multiple image| direction=horizontal| width=| footer=| width1=200| image1=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke front side.jpg| alt1=| caption1=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (front side)| width2=216| image2=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke back side.jpg| alt2=| caption2=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (back side) "Itagaki may die, but liberty never!"}} In [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]], spirit tablets, known as “lotus seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=蓮位}}) for the dead and “prosperity seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=祿位}}) for the living, are used in the same manner for ancestors, wandering spirits, demons, [[:en:Hungry ghosts|Hungry ghosts]], and the living (for the perpetual or temporary blessing of the donor). Temporary tablets in the form of paper are common around the time of Qingming and Ullambana dharma festivals, which are incinerated en masse at the culmination of these services. In [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]], tablets are used in [[:en:Funeral rites|Funeral rites]] and stored in the home ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]''. Tablets are also common in [[:en:buddhist temples in japan|Japanese temples]]. In Korean culture, spirit tablets are of great importance in ancestral rites called ''[[:en:Jesa|Jesa]]'', as they are the centerpieces of food offerings and represent the spiritual presence of the deceased. ==Gallery== File:HongKongSpiritSeat.jpg|A doorway spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]. File:Memorial Tablet for Spirits in Pingtung County Taiwan.JPG|Tablet in [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] File:祖先牌位.jpg|A stone tablet of a tombstone
(Name has been blurred for protection) File:塔婆供養.jpg|Memorial tablets used in [[:en:Nichiren Shoshu|Nichiren Shoshu]] Buddhism File:平平輔の位牌.JPG|A Japanese spirit tablet in a [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] File:Bài vị.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to emperor [[:en:Lê Trang Tông|Lê Trang Tông]] File:Bài vị Võ Văn Dũng.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Võ Văn Dũng|Võ Văn Dũng]] File:Dai Viet Ancestral Tablets (15242593880).jpg|A Japanese ''[[:en:sotoba|sotoba]]'' tablet dedicated to ancestors in [[:en:Hoian|Hoian]], [[:en:Vietnam|Vietnam]] ==See also== * [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|Ancestor veneration in China]] * [[:en:Veneration of the dead|Veneration of the dead]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] * [[:en:Chinese lineage associations|Chinese lineage associations]] and [[:en:Kongsi|Kongsi]] * [[:en:Chinese kinship|Chinese kinship]] * [[:en:Shigandang|Shigandang]] * [[:en:Ullambana|Ullambana]] * [[:en:Zhizha|Zhizha]] and [[:en:Religious goods store|Religious goods store]] * [[:en:Zhong Yuan Festival|Zhong Yuan Festival]] * [[:en:Zupu|Zupu]] and [[:en:Chinese kin|Chinese kin]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[commons:" to "[[sn:commons:"
revision.text:
[[cdo:Sìng-ciō-bà̤]][[sn:commons:Category:Spirit tablets]][[en:Spirit tablet]][[fr:Tablette ancestrale]][[gan:神主牌]][[id:Papan arwah]][[it:Tavoletta degli spiriti]][[ja:位牌]][[ko:위패]][[ms:Papan roh]][[nl:Vooroudertablet]][[no:Shenzhupai]][[pl:Tabliczka przodka]][[simple:Spirit tablet]][[th:ป้ายวิญญาณ]][[tr:Ruh tableti]][[uk:Таблички предків]][[zh_classical:神位]][[zh_yue:神主牌]][[zh:牌位]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1375504]] Wikidata name: spirit tablet [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Ritual object in East Asian ancestor veneration}} {{Use American English|date = January 2019}} {{Use mdy dates|date = January 2019}} {{Infobox Chinese | pic = YuKiuAncestralHall06.jpg | piccap = Spirit tablets for ancestors in Hong Kong | c = 神主牌 | j = san4 zyu2 paai4 | y = sàhn jyú pàaih | p = shénzhǔpái | poj = sîn-chú-pâi | l = spirit master sign | kanji = 位牌 | revhep = ihai | hangul = 1. 위패
2. 신위 | hanja = 1. 位牌
2. 神位 | qn = bài vị | chuhan = 牌位 | rr = 1. wipae
2.shinwi | c2 = 神位 | j2 = san4 wai6 | y2 = sàhn waih | p2 = shénwèi | poj2 = sîn-ūi | l2 = spirit seat | c3 = 神牌 | j3 = san4 paai4 | y3 = sàhn pàaih | p3 = shénpái | poj3 = sîn-pâi | l3 = spirit sign }} A '''spirit tablet''', '''memorial tablet''', or '''ancestral tablet'''{{cite book|last=Li|first=Xiaoxiang|title=Origins of Chinese people and customs|year=2004|publisher=Asiapac Books|location=Singapore|isbn=978-981-229-384-8|page=130|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=l0R59sYWaCoC|edition=Revised|author2=Fu, Chunjiang|author3=Goh, Geraldine|quote=ancestral tablet}}{{Dead link|date=October 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} is a [[:en:Placard|Placard]] that people used to designate the seat of a deity or past ancestor as well as to enclose it. The name of the deity or the past ancestor is usually inscribed onto the tablet. With origins in [[:en:Chinese culture|traditional Chinese culture]], the spirit tablet is a common sight in many East Asian countries, where forms of [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|ancestor veneration]] are practiced. Spirit tablets are traditional ritual objects commonly seen in temples, shrines, and household altars throughout mainland China and Taiwan.{{cite web|title=Ancestors and Deities: Chinese Spirit Tablets|url=http://anthromuseum.missouri.edu/minigalleries/chinesespirittablets/intro.shtml|work=Museum of Anthropology|publisher=University of Missouri|accessdate=19 September 2011}} ==General usage== {{No sources|date=March 2024|section}} A spirit tablet is often used for deities or [[:en:ancestors|ancestors]] (either generally or specifically: e.g. for a specific relative or for one's entire family tree). Shrines are generally found in and around households (for [[:en:Household deity|household gods]] and ancestors), in [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] for specific deities, or in [[ancestral shrine]]s for the clan's founders and specific ancestors. In each place, there are specific locations for individual spirit tablets for ancestors or one or another particular deity. A spirit tablet acts as an [[:en:Effigy|Effigy]] of a specific deity or ancestor. When used, [[:en:Incense|incense sticks]] or [[:en:joss sticks|joss sticks]] are usually burned before the tablet in some kind of [[:en:brazier|brazier]] or [[:en:Censer|incense holder]]. Sometimes fruit, tea, pastries, or other offertory items are placed near the tablet to offer food to that particular spirit or divinity. In [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese folk religion]] a household will have one or more tablets for specific deities and family ancestors: * One near the front door, and at or around eye level, dedicated to the [[:en:Jade Emperor|Jade Emperor]]. Generally, but not always, this tablet will be above the tablet dedicated to Tudigong. This tablet reads {{zh|labels=no|t=天官賜福|s=天官赐福}}. * Some houses will have a tablet at or near the gate which reads {{zh|labels=no|t=門官福神|s=门官福神}} "this tablet is dedicated to the [[:en:Menshen|Door Gods]]". * One outside the house at the front door on the ground, dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]], an [[:en:earth god|Earth Deity]]. This tablet usually reads {{zh|t=門口土地財神 |s=门口土地财神}} (less commonly {{zh|labels=no|t=門口土地福神|s=门口土地福神}}). * One in the kitchen, dedicated to [[:en:Kitchen God|Zao Jun]], the kitchen god, which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=定福灶君}}. * One which is dedicated to the [[Landlord deity|Landlord god]], Dizhu Shen (similar to Tudigong but not the same). This tablet comes in several forms: the simple form which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=地主神位}}, or a longer, more complex form which comprises two couplets commonly reading {{zh|labels=no|t=前后地主財神，五方五土龍神|s=前後地财神，五方五土龙神}}. * Two in the house, usually at least one in the living room. These tablets will usually be put in a cabinet, similar to a Japanese ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]'' household shrine, and they will be usually for a family's ancestors and some other deity which may or may not be represented by a spirit tablet. In their most simple form the spirit tablets can simply be a piece of red paper with the words written vertically (in mainland China and in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]). More complex forms exist; these could be full, small shrines made of tile, wood, metal or other material; statues and attendants with text; small posters with incense places; and so on. A common form of the tablet for Tudigong (as seen in [[:en:Guangdong|Guangdong]], [[:en:China|China]]), for example, consists of a baked tile which has the core text of the tablet {{zh|labels=no|c=門口土地財神}}, flanked by two additional couplets reading {{zh|labels=no|t=戶納千祥， 門迎百福|s=户纳千祥， 门迎百福}}) meaning something close to "May my household welcome a great deal of auspiciousness, may my doors welcome hundreds of blessings". In [[Taoism]], spirit tablets are often used for ancestors. Sometimes spirit tablets are found before or below statues of deities, which represent the enclosed spirit of the deity. {{Multiple image| direction=horizontal| width=| footer=| width1=200| image1=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke front side.jpg| alt1=| caption1=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (front side)| width2=216| image2=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke back side.jpg| alt2=| caption2=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (back side) "Itagaki may die, but liberty never!"}} In [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]], spirit tablets, known as “lotus seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=蓮位}}) for the dead and “prosperity seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=祿位}}) for the living, are used in the same manner for ancestors, wandering spirits, demons, [[:en:Hungry ghosts|Hungry ghosts]], and the living (for the perpetual or temporary blessing of the donor). Temporary tablets in the form of paper are common around the time of Qingming and Ullambana dharma festivals, which are incinerated en masse at the culmination of these services. In [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]], tablets are used in [[:en:Funeral rites|Funeral rites]] and stored in the home ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]''. Tablets are also common in [[:en:buddhist temples in japan|Japanese temples]]. In Korean culture, spirit tablets are of great importance in ancestral rites called ''[[:en:Jesa|Jesa]]'', as they are the centerpieces of food offerings and represent the spiritual presence of the deceased. ==Gallery== File:HongKongSpiritSeat.jpg|A doorway spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]. File:Memorial Tablet for Spirits in Pingtung County Taiwan.JPG|Tablet in [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] File:祖先牌位.jpg|A stone tablet of a tombstone
(Name has been blurred for protection) File:塔婆供養.jpg|Memorial tablets used in [[:en:Nichiren Shoshu|Nichiren Shoshu]] Buddhism File:平平輔の位牌.JPG|A Japanese spirit tablet in a [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] File:Bài vị.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to emperor [[:en:Lê Trang Tông|Lê Trang Tông]] File:Bài vị Võ Văn Dũng.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Võ Văn Dũng|Võ Văn Dũng]] File:Dai Viet Ancestral Tablets (15242593880).jpg|A Japanese ''[[:en:sotoba|sotoba]]'' tablet dedicated to ancestors in [[:en:Hoian|Hoian]], [[:en:Vietnam|Vietnam]] ==See also== * [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|Ancestor veneration in China]] * [[:en:Veneration of the dead|Veneration of the dead]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] * [[:en:Chinese lineage associations|Chinese lineage associations]] and [[:en:Kongsi|Kongsi]] * [[:en:Chinese kinship|Chinese kinship]] * [[:en:Shigandang|Shigandang]] * [[:en:Ullambana|Ullambana]] * [[:en:Zhizha|Zhizha]] and [[:en:Religious goods store|Religious goods store]] * [[:en:Zhong Yuan Festival|Zhong Yuan Festival]] * [[:en:Zupu|Zupu]] and [[:en:Chinese kin|Chinese kin]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Spirit tablets}}]] {{Religious Confucianism}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancestral shrines]] [[Category:Filial piety]] [[Category:Practices in Chinese folk religion]] [[Category:Religion in Japan]] [[Category:Religion in Korea]] [[Category:Religious Confucianism]] [[Category:Traditional rituals of East Asia]] }} [[en:Spirit tablet]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[cdo:Sìng-ciō-bà̤]] {{Commons category|Spirit tablets}} [[fr:Tablette ancestrale]] [[gan:神主牌]] [[id:Papan arwah]] [[it:Tavoletta degli spiriti]] [[ja:位牌]] [[ko:위패]] [[ms:Papan roh]] [[nl:Vooroudertablet]] [[no:Shenzhupai]] [[pl:Tabliczka przodka]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Spirit tablet]] [[th:ป้ายวิญญาณ]] [[tr:Ruh tableti]] [[Category:Religion in Japan]] [[Category:Religion in Korea]] [[Category:Religious Confucianism]] [[Category:Traditional rituals of East Asia]] }} [[en:Spirit tablet]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[cdo:Sìng-ciō-bà̤]] {{Commons category|Spirit tablets}} [[fr:Tablette ancestrale]] [[gan:神主牌]] [[id:Papan arwah]] [[it:Tavoletta degli spiriti]] [[ja:位牌]] [[ko:위패]] [[ms:Papan roh]] [[nl:Vooroudertablet]] [[no:Shenzhupai]] [[pl:Tabliczka przodka]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Spirit tablet]] [[th:ป้ายวิญญาณ]] [[tr:Ruh tableti]] revision.comment: Text replacement - "zh_classical" to "zh-classical"
revision.text:
[[cdo:Sìng-ciō-bà̤]][[sn:commons:Category:Spirit tablets]][[en:Spirit tablet]][[fr:Tablette ancestrale]][[gan:神主牌]][[id:Papan arwah]][[it:Tavoletta degli spiriti]][[ja:位牌]][[ko:위패]][[ms:Papan roh]][[nl:Vooroudertablet]][[no:Shenzhupai]][[pl:Tabliczka przodka]][[simple:Spirit tablet]][[th:ป้ายวิญญาณ]][[tr:Ruh tableti]][[uk:Таблички предків]][[zh-classical:神位]][[zh-yue:神主牌]][[zh:牌位]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1375504]] Wikidata name: spirit tablet [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Ritual object in East Asian ancestor veneration}} {{Use American English|date = January 2019}} {{Use mdy dates|date = January 2019}} {{Infobox Chinese | pic = YuKiuAncestralHall06.jpg | piccap = Spirit tablets for ancestors in Hong Kong | c = 神主牌 | j = san4 zyu2 paai4 | y = sàhn jyú pàaih | p = shénzhǔpái | poj = sîn-chú-pâi | l = spirit master sign | kanji = 位牌 | revhep = ihai | hangul = 1. 위패
2. 신위 | hanja = 1. 位牌
2. 神位 | qn = bài vị | chuhan = 牌位 | rr = 1. wipae
2.shinwi | c2 = 神位 | j2 = san4 wai6 | y2 = sàhn waih | p2 = shénwèi | poj2 = sîn-ūi | l2 = spirit seat | c3 = 神牌 | j3 = san4 paai4 | y3 = sàhn pàaih | p3 = shénpái | poj3 = sîn-pâi | l3 = spirit sign }} A '''spirit tablet''', '''memorial tablet''', or '''ancestral tablet'''{{cite book|last=Li|first=Xiaoxiang|title=Origins of Chinese people and customs|year=2004|publisher=Asiapac Books|location=Singapore|isbn=978-981-229-384-8|page=130|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=l0R59sYWaCoC|edition=Revised|author2=Fu, Chunjiang|author3=Goh, Geraldine|quote=ancestral tablet}}{{Dead link|date=October 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} is a [[:en:Placard|Placard]] that people used to designate the seat of a deity or past ancestor as well as to enclose it. The name of the deity or the past ancestor is usually inscribed onto the tablet. With origins in [[:en:Chinese culture|traditional Chinese culture]], the spirit tablet is a common sight in many East Asian countries, where forms of [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|ancestor veneration]] are practiced. Spirit tablets are traditional ritual objects commonly seen in temples, shrines, and household altars throughout mainland China and Taiwan.{{cite web|title=Ancestors and Deities: Chinese Spirit Tablets|url=http://anthromuseum.missouri.edu/minigalleries/chinesespirittablets/intro.shtml|work=Museum of Anthropology|publisher=University of Missouri|accessdate=19 September 2011}} ==General usage== {{No sources|date=March 2024|section}} A spirit tablet is often used for deities or [[:en:ancestors|ancestors]] (either generally or specifically: e.g. for a specific relative or for one's entire family tree). Shrines are generally found in and around households (for [[:en:Household deity|household gods]] and ancestors), in [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] for specific deities, or in [[ancestral shrine]]s for the clan's founders and specific ancestors. In each place, there are specific locations for individual spirit tablets for ancestors or one or another particular deity. A spirit tablet acts as an [[:en:Effigy|Effigy]] of a specific deity or ancestor. When used, [[:en:Incense|incense sticks]] or [[:en:joss sticks|joss sticks]] are usually burned before the tablet in some kind of [[:en:brazier|brazier]] or [[:en:Censer|incense holder]]. Sometimes fruit, tea, pastries, or other offertory items are placed near the tablet to offer food to that particular spirit or divinity. In [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese folk religion]] a household will have one or more tablets for specific deities and family ancestors: * One near the front door, and at or around eye level, dedicated to the [[:en:Jade Emperor|Jade Emperor]]. Generally, but not always, this tablet will be above the tablet dedicated to Tudigong. This tablet reads {{zh|labels=no|t=天官賜福|s=天官赐福}}. * Some houses will have a tablet at or near the gate which reads {{zh|labels=no|t=門官福神|s=门官福神}} "this tablet is dedicated to the [[:en:Menshen|Door Gods]]". * One outside the house at the front door on the ground, dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]], an [[:en:earth god|Earth Deity]]. This tablet usually reads {{zh|t=門口土地財神 |s=门口土地财神}} (less commonly {{zh|labels=no|t=門口土地福神|s=门口土地福神}}). * One in the kitchen, dedicated to [[:en:Kitchen God|Zao Jun]], the kitchen god, which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=定福灶君}}. * One which is dedicated to the [[Landlord deity|Landlord god]], Dizhu Shen (similar to Tudigong but not the same). This tablet comes in several forms: the simple form which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=地主神位}}, or a longer, more complex form which comprises two couplets commonly reading {{zh|labels=no|t=前后地主財神，五方五土龍神|s=前後地财神，五方五土龙神}}. * Two in the house, usually at least one in the living room. These tablets will usually be put in a cabinet, similar to a Japanese ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]'' household shrine, and they will be usually for a family's ancestors and some other deity which may or may not be represented by a spirit tablet. In their most simple form the spirit tablets can simply be a piece of red paper with the words written vertically (in mainland China and in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]). More complex forms exist; these could be full, small shrines made of tile, wood, metal or other material; statues and attendants with text; small posters with incense places; and so on. A common form of the tablet for Tudigong (as seen in [[:en:Guangdong|Guangdong]], [[:en:China|China]]), for example, consists of a baked tile which has the core text of the tablet {{zh|labels=no|c=門口土地財神}}, flanked by two additional couplets reading {{zh|labels=no|t=戶納千祥， 門迎百福|s=户纳千祥， 门迎百福}}) meaning something close to "May my household welcome a great deal of auspiciousness, may my doors welcome hundreds of blessings". In [[Taoism]], spirit tablets are often used for ancestors. Sometimes spirit tablets are found before or below statues of deities, which represent the enclosed spirit of the deity. {{Multiple image| direction=horizontal| width=| footer=| width1=200| image1=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke front side.jpg| alt1=| caption1=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (front side)| width2=216| image2=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke back side.jpg| alt2=| caption2=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (back side) "Itagaki may die, but liberty never!"}} In [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]], spirit tablets, known as “lotus seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=蓮位}}) for the dead and “prosperity seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=祿位}}) for the living, are used in the same manner for ancestors, wandering spirits, demons, [[:en:Hungry ghosts|Hungry ghosts]], and the living (for the perpetual or temporary blessing of the donor). Temporary tablets in the form of paper are common around the time of Qingming and Ullambana dharma festivals, which are incinerated en masse at the culmination of these services. In [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]], tablets are used in [[:en:Funeral rites|Funeral rites]] and stored in the home ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]''. Tablets are also common in [[:en:buddhist temples in japan|Japanese temples]]. In Korean culture, spirit tablets are of great importance in ancestral rites called ''[[:en:Jesa|Jesa]]'', as they are the centerpieces of food offerings and represent the spiritual presence of the deceased. ==Gallery== File:HongKongSpiritSeat.jpg|A doorway spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]. File:Memorial Tablet for Spirits in Pingtung County Taiwan.JPG|Tablet in [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] File:祖先牌位.jpg|A stone tablet of a tombstone
(Name has been blurred for protection) File:塔婆供養.jpg|Memorial tablets used in [[:en:Nichiren Shoshu|Nichiren Shoshu]] Buddhism File:平平輔の位牌.JPG|A Japanese spirit tablet in a [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] File:Bài vị.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to emperor [[:en:Lê Trang Tông|Lê Trang Tông]] File:Bài vị Võ Văn Dũng.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Võ Văn Dũng|Võ Văn Dũng]] File:Dai Viet Ancestral Tablets (15242593880).jpg|A Japanese ''[[:en:sotoba|sotoba]]'' tablet dedicated to ancestors in [[:en:Hoian|Hoian]], [[:en:Vietnam|Vietnam]] ==See also== * [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|Ancestor veneration in China]] * [[:en:Veneration of the dead|Veneration of the dead]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] * [[:en:Chinese lineage associations|Chinese lineage associations]] and [[:en:Kongsi|Kongsi]] * [[:en:Chinese kinship|Chinese kinship]] * [[:en:Shigandang|Shigandang]] * [[:en:Ullambana|Ullambana]] * [[:en:Zhizha|Zhizha]] and [[:en:Religious goods store|Religious goods store]] * [[:en:Zhong Yuan Festival|Zhong Yuan Festival]] * [[:en:Zupu|Zupu]] and [[:en:Chinese kin|Chinese kin]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Spirit tablets}}]] {{Religious Confucianism}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Ancestral shrines]] [[Category:Filial piety]] [[Category:Practices in Chinese folk religion]] [[Category:Religion in Japan]] [[Category:Religion in Korea]] [[Category:Religious Confucianism]] [[Category:Traditional rituals of East Asia]] }} [[en:Spirit tablet]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[cdo:Sìng-ciō-bà̤]] {{Commons category|Spirit tablets}} [[fr:Tablette ancestrale]] [[gan:神主牌]] [[id:Papan arwah]] [[it:Tavoletta degli spiriti]] [[ja:位牌]] [[ko:위패]] [[ms:Papan roh]] [[nl:Vooroudertablet]] [[no:Shenzhupai]] [[pl:Tabliczka przodka]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Spirit tablet]] [[th:ป้ายวิญญาณ]] [[tr:Ruh tableti]] [[uk:Таблички предків]] [[zh:牌位]] [[zh-classical:神位]] [[zh-yue:神主牌]] {{wikidata link|Q1375504}}

The name of the deity or the past ancestor is usually inscribed onto the tablet. With origins in [[:en:Chinese culture|traditional Chinese culture]], the spirit tablet is a common sight in many East Asian countries, where forms of [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|ancestor veneration]] are practiced. Spirit tablets are traditional ritual objects commonly seen in temples, shrines, and household altars throughout mainland China and Taiwan.{{cite web|title=Ancestors and Deities: Chinese Spirit Tablets|url=http://anthromuseum.missouri.edu/minigalleries/chinesespirittablets/intro.shtml|work=Museum of Anthropology|publisher=University of Missouri|accessdate=19 September 2011}} ==General usage== {{No sources|date=March 2024|section}} A spirit tablet is often used for deities or [[:en:ancestors|ancestors]] (either generally or specifically: e.g. for a specific relative or for one's entire family tree). Shrines are generally found in and around households (for [[:en:Household deity|household gods]] and ancestors), in [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] for specific deities, or in [[ancestral shrine]]s for the clan's founders and specific ancestors. In each place, there are specific locations for individual spirit tablets for ancestors or one or another particular deity. A spirit tablet acts as an [[:en:Effigy|Effigy]] of a specific deity or ancestor. When used, [[:en:Incense|incense sticks]] or [[:en:joss sticks|joss sticks]] are usually burned before the tablet in some kind of [[:en:brazier|brazier]] or [[:en:Censer|incense holder]]. Sometimes fruit, tea, pastries, or other offertory items are placed near the tablet to offer food to that particular spirit or divinity. In [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese folk religion]] a household will have one or more tablets for specific deities and family ancestors: * One near the front door, and at or around eye level, dedicated to the [[:en:Jade Emperor|Jade Emperor]]. Generally, but not always, this tablet will be above the tablet dedicated to Tudigong. This tablet reads {{zh|labels=no|t=天官賜福|s=天官赐福}}. * Some houses will have a tablet at or near the gate which reads {{zh|labels=no|t=門官福神|s=门官福神}} "this tablet is dedicated to the [[:en:Menshen|Door Gods]]". * One outside the house at the front door on the ground, dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]], an [[:en:earth god|Earth Deity]]. This tablet usually reads {{zh|t=門口土地財神 |s=门口土地财神}} (less commonly {{zh|labels=no|t=門口土地福神|s=门口土地福神}}). * One in the kitchen, dedicated to [[:en:Kitchen God|Zao Jun]], the kitchen god, which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=定福灶君}}. * One which is dedicated to the [[Landlord deity|Landlord god]], Dizhu Shen (similar to Tudigong but not the same). This tablet comes in several forms: the simple form which reads {{zh|labels=no|c=地主神位}}, or a longer, more complex form which comprises two couplets commonly reading {{zh|labels=no|t=前后地主財神，五方五土龍神|s=前後地财神，五方五土龙神}}. * Two in the house, usually at least one in the living room. These tablets will usually be put in a cabinet, similar to a Japanese ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]'' household shrine, and they will be usually for a family's ancestors and some other deity which may or may not be represented by a spirit tablet. In their most simple form the spirit tablets can simply be a piece of red paper with the words written vertically (in mainland China and in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]). More complex forms exist; these could be full, small shrines made of tile, wood, metal or other material; statues and attendants with text; small posters with incense places; and so on. A common form of the tablet for Tudigong (as seen in [[:en:Guangdong|Guangdong]], [[:en:China|China]]), for example, consists of a baked tile which has the core text of the tablet {{zh|labels=no|c=門口土地財神}}, flanked by two additional couplets reading {{zh|labels=no|t=戶納千祥， 門迎百福|s=户纳千祥， 门迎百福}}) meaning something close to "May my household welcome a great deal of auspiciousness, may my doors welcome hundreds of blessings". In [[Taoism]], spirit tablets are often used for ancestors. Sometimes spirit tablets are found before or below statues of deities, which represent the enclosed spirit of the deity. {{Multiple image| direction=horizontal| width=| footer=| width1=200| image1=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke front side.jpg| alt1=| caption1=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (front side)| width2=216| image2=Buddhist memorial tablet of Itagaki Taisuke back side.jpg| alt2=| caption2=Buddhist memorial tablet of [[:en:Itagaki Taisuke|Itagaki Taisuke]] (back side) "Itagaki may die, but liberty never!"}} In [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]], spirit tablets, known as “lotus seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=蓮位}}) for the dead and “prosperity seats” ({{zh|labels=no|c=祿位}}) for the living, are used in the same manner for ancestors, wandering spirits, demons, [[:en:Hungry ghosts|Hungry ghosts]], and the living (for the perpetual or temporary blessing of the donor). Temporary tablets in the form of paper are common around the time of Qingming and Ullambana dharma festivals, which are incinerated en masse at the culmination of these services. In [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Japanese Buddhism]], tablets are used in [[:en:Funeral rites|Funeral rites]] and stored in the home ''[[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]]''. Tablets are also common in [[:en:buddhist temples in japan|Japanese temples]]. In Korean culture, spirit tablets are of great importance in ancestral rites called ''[[:en:Jesa|Jesa]]'', as they are the centerpieces of food offerings and represent the spiritual presence of the deceased. ==Gallery== File:HongKongSpiritSeat.jpg|A doorway spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Tudigong|Tudigong]] in [[:en:Hong Kong|Hong Kong]]. File:Memorial Tablet for Spirits in Pingtung County Taiwan.JPG|Tablet in [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]] File:祖先牌位.jpg|A stone tablet of a tombstone
(Name has been blurred for protection) File:塔婆供養.jpg|Memorial tablets used in [[:en:Nichiren Shoshu|Nichiren Shoshu]] Buddhism File:平平輔の位牌.JPG|A Japanese spirit tablet in a [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] File:Bài vị.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to emperor [[:en:Lê Trang Tông|Lê Trang Tông]] File:Bài vị Võ Văn Dũng.jpg|A Vietnamese spirit tablet dedicated to [[:en:Võ Văn Dũng|Võ Văn Dũng]] File:Dai Viet Ancestral Tablets (15242593880).jpg|A Japanese ''[[:en:sotoba|sotoba]]'' tablet dedicated to ancestors in [[:en:Hoian|Hoian]], [[:en:Vietnam|Vietnam]] ==See also== * [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|Ancestor veneration in China]] * [[:en:Veneration of the dead|Veneration of the dead]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Butsudan|Butsudan]] * [[:en:Chinese lineage associations|Chinese lineage associations]] and [[:en:Kongsi|Kongsi]] * [[:en:Chinese kinship|Chinese kinship]] * [[:en:Shigandang|Shigandang]] * [[:en:Ullambana|Ullambana]] * [[:en:Zhizha|Zhizha]] and [[:en:Religious goods store|Religious goods store]] * [[:en:Zhong Yuan Festival|Zhong Yuan Festival]] * [[:en:Zupu|Zupu]] and [[:en:Chinese kin|Chinese kin]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External 2. 신위 | hanja = 1. 位牌
2. 神位 | qn = bài vị | chuhan = 牌位 | rr = 1. wipae
2.shinwi | c2 = 神位 | j2 = san4 wai6 | y2 = sàhn waih | p2 = shénwèi | poj2 = sîn-ūi | l2 = spirit seat | c3 = 神牌 | j3 = san4 paai4 | y3 = sàhn pàaih | p3 = shénpái | poj3 = sîn-pâi | l3 = spirit sign }} A '''spirit tablet''', '''memorial tablet''', or '''ancestral tablet'''{{cite book|last=Li|first=Xiaoxiang|title=Origins of Chinese people and customs|year=2004|publisher=Asiapac Books|location=Singapore|isbn=978-981-229-384-8|page=130|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=l0R59sYWaCoC|edition=Revised|author2=Fu, Chunjiang|author3=Goh, Geraldine|quote=ancestral tablet}}{{Dead link|date=October 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} is a [[:en:Placard|Placard]] that people used to designate the seat of a deity or past ancestor as well as to enclose it. The name of the deity or the past ancestor is usually inscribed onto the tablet. With origins in [[:en:Chinese culture|traditional Chinese culture]], the spirit tablet is a common sight in many East Asian countries, where forms of [[:en:Ancestor veneration in China|ancestor veneration]] are practiced.
